title: Ohm's Law — Rules 1–12
source: Leith, G. (1967). What Is Programmed Learning? BBC Publications.

propositions.text: An electric charge is produced by friction.
The electron is the basic unit of charge.
The electron is an impractical unit of charge.
The coulomb is the practical unit of charge.
Current is a flow of charge.
Current is measured in coulombs per second.
One coulomb per second is called an ampere.
When current flows, work is done.
Energy is used when work is done.
Energy must be supplied for current to flow.
EMF measures the rate at which energy is supplied.

relations.justification: Both establish the concept of electric charge.
Both address the electron as a unit.
Electron vs coulomb: practical vs impractical unit.
Direct contrast: impractical vs practical unit.
Current as flow of charge requires prior definition of charge unit.
Measuring current in coulombs/sec requires understanding current.
Ampere is defined as one coulomb per second.
Current flow is associated with work being done.
Energy use depends on understanding that work is done.
Energy must be supplied follows from energy being used.
EMF is defined in terms of energy supply rate.
Both concern energy; joules measure the energy in rule 9.
EMF rate is measured in units related to joules.
Current flow and work done are associated phenomena.
Rate of charge flow associated with rate of energy use.
